title: Electrically driven Rabi dynamics of magnetic-field-induced corner states in a two-dimensional topological insulator
authors: D. V. Khomitsky, E. A. Lavrukhina, D. P. Krasavin, S. S. Krishtopenko, F. Teppe
abstract: We study coherent electric manipulation of magnetic-field-induced localized states at a double kink of a helical edge in a HgTe/CdHgTe quantum well. An in-plane magnetic field opens a gap in the one-dimensional edge spectrum, while changes in the edge orientation generate localized in-gap states at the kinks. We show that, for suitable geometry and magnetic-field direction, two such states form an effective lithographically defined two-level subsystem. Using an edge-state model that includes both the localized levels and the continuum states outside the magnetic-field-induced gap, we calculate the electric-dipole matrix elements and solve the time-dependent problem under resonant driving. The resulting dynamics exhibits Rabi oscillations with linear frequencies of $20$--$40$~GHz for realistic parameters. We find that the continuum states provide a leakage channel whose strength is strongly controlled by the driving amplitude: reducing the electric field suppresses leakage below the percent level while preserving GHz-scale coherent oscillations. These results establish a route from magnetic-field-induced corner-state physics to electrically driven two-level dynamics in a realistic two-dimensional topological-insulator edge geometry.
summary: This paper explores how to use electric fields to drive coherent Rabi oscillations between localized corner states in a 2D topological insulator. It identifies an optimal regime where high-frequency manipulation is possible while keeping population loss to the edge-state continuum extremely low. This provides a potential pathway for creating lithographically defined, electrically controllable qubits.
main_problem: Investigating the feasibility of using magnetic-field-induced corner states in a 2D topological insulator as a controllable two-level subsystem (qubit) driven by electric fields.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that GHz-scale Rabi oscillations can be achieved with leakage into the continuum suppressed below 1% by optimizing the electric field amplitude.
method: The study uses an effective edge-state model to solve the time-dependent Schrödinger equation, explicitly accounting for both discrete localized levels and the continuum of edge states.
model_or_system: A 1D edge of a HgTe/CdHgTe quantum well with a double-kink geometry, subject to an in-plane magnetic field that induces a gap and localized states.
key_observables: Rabi frequencies, population of discrete levels, leakage probability into the continuum, and oscillations in the mean coordinate and spin projection.
important_parameters: Rabi frequencies of 20-40 GHz, electric field amplitudes of 0.05-0.1 ueV/nm, and Zeeman energy scales.
main_assumptions: The edge orientation is piecewise constant, the magnetic field is strictly in-plane, and the outer edge segments are sufficiently long.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the physical geometry of the kinked edge; Figures 6 and 7 illustrate the time-dependent populations, leakage, and spin/spatial oscillations under different electric field strengths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and Hamiltonian, derives the localized states and their dipole matrix elements, and then performs time-dependent simulations to analyze the trade-off between driving strength and population leakage.
why_interesting: It presents a concrete realization of an electrically driven two-level system where the competition between coherent driving and leakage into a continuum can be precisely controlled, relevant to open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of a HgTe/CdHgTe QW edge containing two lithographically defined kinks, which divide the edge into regions 1, 2, and 3. The orientations of the three edge seg- ments are specified by the angles θ1,2,3 measured with respect to the crystallographic direction (100). The central segment lies along the x direction and extends from x = 0 to x = L. The in-plane magnetic field B forms an angle ϕ with the (100) direction. The full edge is truncated at a length Linf ≫L.
FIG. 2. Schematic of the spectrum consisting of two con- tinuum branches described by Eq. (7). The magnetic field opens a common gap 2∆B in the edge spectrum, with ∆B = minn p

FIG. 4. (a) Probability-density distributions |Ψ|2 for the lev- els E1,2 marked in Fig. 3. The two states are localized near the left and right ends of the central segment 0 < x < L in Fig. 1. (b) Absolute value of the dipole matrix element (16) as a function of the angles θ1 = θ3 and ϕ. The marked value |X12| = 885 nm corresponds to the selected pair of discrete levels whose probability-density distributions are shown in panel (a).

FIG. 3. (a) Spectrum of the two discrete levels E1,2 shown schematically in Fig. 2 as a function of the angles θ1 = θ3 and ϕ defined in Fig. 1. The other parameters are θ2 = 0, L = 250 nm, and EZ = 150 µeV. The uniform background regions at the top and bottom correspond to the absence of a pair of localized levels. The selected levels E1,2 = ∓1.342 EZ at θ1 = θ3 = 2π/3 and ϕ = π/2 are marked by black circles. (b), (c) Distributions of the x component of spin for the lower (b) and upper (c) levels shown in panel (a). For the selected pair of levels, the x-spin projections have opposite signs and nearly equal magnitudes.

FIG. 5. Absolute value of the matrix element (23) for tran- sitions into continuum states of the family Ψ(L): (a) lower continuum branch, −nb∆B < E < −∆B, and (b) upper con- tinuum branch, ∆B < E < nb∆B, with nb = 200. Each panel shows transitions from the lower discrete level E1 and from the upper discrete level E2.
